annotation_target.structure: Fugue exposition — entries stack the texture from two voices to four (soprano answer m37, tenor m39, bass m40)
key_moments: m37 soprano answer at the dominant (subject shape starting on G)
m39 tenor entry on G, an octave below the soprano's answer
m40 bass entry on C completes the four-voice texture
teaching_goals: track each new voice as it enters while the earlier ones keep moving
balance independent lines — the entering voice leads, the others accompany
style_tips: slightly favor whichever voice has the subject
keep the eighth-note pulse steady as the texture thickens

teaching_notes.note: The tenor entry sits in the middle of the texture and is easy to lose — bring it out.
technique: keep the upper lines lighter here

teaching_notes.note: The bass entry completes the exposition — the fugue is fully assembled from here on.

target_trace.objective: Read mm. 37–40 of Prelude in C Major, BWV 846 (Well-Tempered Clavier) (continuation of mm. 33–36), analyze the musical continuation, play in a loop.

session.content: Play measures 37–40 and describe how the fugue exposition builds.
Let me view the piano roll for mm. 37–40.

Measures 37–40 are the heart of the fugue's exposition. The soprano answers at the dominant in m.37 (the subject shape starting on G), the tenor enters on G an octave lower in m.39, and the bass completes the four-voice texture with the subject on C in m.40. Each entry adds a genuinely independent line — by m.40 the note density has nearly doubled, and F# inflections color the answer entries. Let me loop mm. 37–40.
Bach BWV 846 fugue mm. 37–40: exposition — soprano answer (m37), tenor entry (m39), bass entry (m40); the texture grows from two voices to four. Continuation from mm. 33–36.

eval_metadata.split_strategy: stratified by (composer, composition_id) with MIDI byte-hash dedup
